common_name: Corno di Toro Pepper (Bulls Horn Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Corno di Toro ('Bull's Horn' in Italian) is a traditional Italian heirloom frying pepper producing long, curved, tapered, horn-shaped fruits — bright red or golden yellow — with thin walls, very sweet, rich flavor, and outstanding quality for roasting, grilling, and sautéing; one of the most important Italian kitchen garden peppers.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 2 weeks after frost into warm, well-drained, fertile soil. Corno di Toro ('Bull's Horn') is a traditional Italian heirloom sweet pepper used throughout Italian cooking as the primary roasting and frying pepper; it comes in both red (rosso) and yellow (giallo) forms that are harvested when fully colored. The long (6–10 inch), tapering, distinctively curved and horn-shaped fruits have thin, tender walls with very sweet, slightly smoky flavor that intensifies beautifully when roasted under a broiler or on a grill; the thin skin blisters and removes easily when charred. Used extensively in Italian cuisine for peperonata, antipasto, stuffed peppers, and as a roasted sandwich pepper. The sweet, rich flavor makes Corno di Toro superior to blocky bell peppers for roasting and many cooked preparations. A productive plant that bears heavily through summer; excellent for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Antohi Romanian is a distinctly early, mild, sweet frying pepper introduced to North American gardeners by Romanian dissident Silviu Antohi, who brought seeds from Romania to Canada in the 1980s — producing pale yellow to orange, blocky to elongated, thin-walled fruits with exceptional sweetness and very early production for a sweet pepper.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Antohi Romanian is notably earlier than most sweet peppers and can be productive even in short-season gardens. Antohi Romanian pepper arrived in North American heirloom seed circles through Romanian dissident Silviu Antohi, who escaped Romania during the Communist period and brought pepper seeds with him that he shared with gardeners and seed savers in Canada; the variety entered the Seed Savers Exchange network in the 1990s and has since become widely appreciated for its exceptional earliness and high quality. The medium-sized (3–4 inch), blocky to elongated-oval, pale yellow to orange fruits have thin walls and an exceptionally sweet, fruity flavor with virtually no bitterness; they are among the earliest sweet peppers to begin producing, with plants in production 60–72 days from transplant — significantly earlier than most sweet bell types. Outstanding fresh, roasted, or pickled; the thin walls cook down quickly and the fruits need no peeling when roasted. A highly productive plant for short-season gardens and an outstanding choice for Gulf Coast early-season plantings.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; sweet paprika pepper is a Hungarian/Central European cultivar group bred specifically for drying and grinding into paprika powder, with extraordinarily high carotenoid content giving both the brilliant red color and the sweet, distinctive paprika flavor — grown domestically to produce fresh, vivid paprika far superior in flavor to commercial dried paprika.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; sweet paprika peppers are harvested when fully red and then dried whole (traditionally strung on twine and air-dried) or split and dehydrated before grinding. Sweet (édes) paprika peppers are a cultivar group of Capsicum annuum developed in Hungary for the production of paprika spice, the defining seasoning of Hungarian cuisine and a cornerstone of Central and Eastern European cooking; the peppers themselves are characterized by exceptionally high carotenoid content (primarily capsanthin and capsorubin) that produces both the brilliant deep red color of dried paprika and its characteristic sweet, slightly fruity, mildly smoky flavor with zero heat. Fresh sweet paprika peppers can be eaten like any sweet bell pepper — they have a somewhat thicker, crunchier texture and a distinctive paprika aroma even when raw. For making paprika powder, dry the fully ripe red fruits at 140–160°F until brittle, then grind; freshly ground home paprika is dramatically more vivid in color and flavor than commercial imported paprika.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Calabrian peperoncino is the foundational hot chile of southern Italian cuisine, particularly from Calabria (the 'toe' of Italy's boot) — small, round to slightly elongated, intensely red, moderately hot (25,000–40,000 SHU) fruits used dried, in olive oil, as chili flakes, and as the spicy heat element in Calabrian cuisine.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Calabrian peperoncino benefits from a long, hot growing season and thrives in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens where it often behaves as a perennial in zone 9–11. Calabrian peperoncino (piccante calabrese, diavolicchio) is the defining hot chile of Calabria in southern Italy, where it has been grown for centuries as the primary heat element of the local cuisine; the small (1–2 inch), round to conical, bright red fruits (at 25,000–40,000 Scoville) are moderately hot with a fruity, complex flavor that distinguishes them from the clean heat of cayenne or the fruity intensity of habanero. Calabrian peperoncino is most commonly used dried — strung in traditional pepper braids (sporte di peperoncini) to air-dry in the Mediterranean sun — and is used throughout Italian cuisine as 'nduja filling (the spreadable Calabrian salumi), peperoncino in olio (chiles preserved in olive oil), as crushed dried flakes on pizza, pasta, and grilled meats, and as chili paste. The plants are highly productive and the small fruits dry readily on the vine or indoors.
sources: Italian Ministry of Agricultural Heirloom Variety Registry

common_name: Marconi Red Sweet Pepper (Italian Frying Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Marconi Red is a traditional Italian sweet frying pepper producing long (6–8 inch), slender, deeply ribbed, bright red fruits with thin walls and exceptional sweet, rich flavor — outstanding for frying in olive oil, roasting, and sautéing, used throughout Italian cuisine where its sweetness intensifies dramatically with heat.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Marconi is productive and begins bearing early for a long sweet pepper; the fruits can be harvested green or left to ripen fully red for the sweetest, richest flavor. Marconi Red is an Italian heirloom sweet frying pepper producing long, tapering, deeply lobed, 6–8 inch fruits that ripen from green to brilliant red; unlike blocky bell peppers, Marconi has thin, tender walls with very sweet, rich, slightly smoky pepper flavor that intensifies dramatically when fried in olive oil or roasted over a flame. Used throughout Italian and Mediterranean cooking as the classic 'frying pepper' for peperonata, antipasto, and pan-fried pepper dishes. The thin walls mean the fruits cook quickly and evenly without becoming tough; they also need minimal or no skin removal after roasting. Marconi is one of several related Italian frying pepper varieties (along with Corno di Toro and Jimmy Nardello) that together represent the tradition of Italian long sweet peppers, each with slightly different fruit shape, texture, and flavor profile. Very productive plants set fruit continuously through summer.
